tasks.title: Warm-up: Create a Headless Service and Verify the ClusterIP
tasks.goal: Confirm that a Headless Service declared with clusterIP: None is created without a virtual IP. Compare it side-by-side with a regular ClusterIP Service to experience the difference firsthand.
tasks.prompt:
Create the following two Services.

Service A (regular): name=nginx-normal, selector=app:nginx, port 80, type omitted (ClusterIP default)
Service B (Headless): name=nginx-headless, selector=app:nginx, port 80, clusterIP: None

After creating both, compare the CLUSTER-IP column in kubectl get svc and explain the difference between the two Services.
tasks.starter:
# nginx-normal-svc.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-normal
spec:
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      name: web

---
# nginx-headless-svc.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: ____    # the single line that makes it Headless
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      name: web
tasks.solution:
# nginx-normal-svc.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-normal
spec:
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      name: web

# nginx-headless-svc.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      name: web

# Apply
kubectl apply -f nginx-normal-svc.yaml
kubectl apply -f nginx-headless-svc.yaml

# Compare
kubectl get svc nginx-normal nginx-headless
# Expected output:
# NAME             TYPE        CLUSTER-IP      PORT(S)   AGE
# nginx-normal     ClusterIP   10.96.xxx.xxx   80/TCP    10s
# nginx-headless   ClusterIP   None            80/TCP    8s
tasks.explanation: Setting clusterIP: None is all it takes to create a Headless Service. A regular Service receives a virtual IP (ClusterIP) backed by iptables/IPVS rules and acts as a load balancer. A Headless Service skips the virtual IP entirely and instead registers individual A records for each backing Pod in DNS. When a StatefulSet references this Service, each Pod gets a stable DNS address in the form web-0.nginx-headless.default.svc.cluster.local, allowing clients to target a specific Pod directly.
hints: The clusterIP field sits directly under spec, at the same level as selector and ports.
In kubectl get svc output, a CLUSTER-IP value of None confirms success. If an IP address appears instead, double-check that clusterIP: None was entered correctly.
The clusterIP of an existing Service cannot be changed. If you need to fix it, delete the Service with kubectl delete svc nginx-headless and re-apply.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Deploy a StatefulSet and Observe Ordered Pod Creation
tasks.goal: Write and deploy a StatefulSet manifest that includes volumeClaimTemplates. Use -w (watch) to observe Pods being created one at a time in the order web-0 → web-1 → web-2. Verify the PVC naming convention as well.
tasks.prompt:
Write a StatefulSet manifest (web-statefulset.yaml) that uses the nginx-headless Service created in Step 1.
- name: web, replicas: 3, serviceName: nginx-headless
- Container: nginx:1.25, port 80, mount the data volume at /usr/share/nginx/html
- volumeClaimTemplates: name=data, accessModes=ReadWriteOnce, storage=1Gi

After applying, verify the following two things.
(a) Run kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w and observe that Pods are created strictly in order.
(b) Run kubectl get pvc and confirm that three PVCs — data-web-0, data-web-1, data-web-2 — were automatically provisioned.
tasks.starter:
# web-statefulset.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  serviceName: ____          # name of the Headless Service
  replicas: ____
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:1.25
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
              name: web
          volumeMounts:
            - name: ____      # must match the name in volumeClaimTemplates
              mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: ____
      spec:
        accessModes: ["____"]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: ____
tasks.solution:
# web-statefulset.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  serviceName: nginx-headless
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:1.25
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
              name: web
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 1Gi

# Apply
kubectl apply -f web-statefulset.yaml

# (a) Observe ordered creation (run in a separate terminal)
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w
# Expected output:
# NAME    READY   STATUS              AGE
# web-0   0/1     ContainerCreating   2s
# web-0   1/1     Running             8s   <- web-1 not created until web-0 is Ready
# web-1   0/1     ContainerCreating   9s
# web-1   1/1     Running             15s  <- web-2 not created until web-1 is Ready
# web-2   0/1     ContainerCreating   16s
# web-2   1/1     Running             22s

# (b) Verify automatic PVC provisioning
kubectl get pvc
# Expected output:
# NAME        STATUS   VOLUME       CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES
# data-web-0  Bound    pvc-xxxxxxx  1Gi        RWO
# data-web-1  Bound    pvc-xxxxxxx  1Gi        RWO
# data-web-2  Bound    pvc-xxxxxxx  1Gi        RWO
tasks.explanation: In a StatefulSet spec, volumeClaimTemplates is a blueprint, not a volumes entry. Each time Kubernetes creates a Pod, it uses this blueprint to provision a PVC named <template-name>-<pod-name>. As you can see in the watch output, web-1 is not created until web-0 reaches Running and Ready — this is the Ordered (sequential) behavior that sets StatefulSet apart from Deployment. The serviceName field must exactly match an existing Headless Service name so that DNS records are created correctly for each Pod.
hints: volumeMounts.name and volumeClaimTemplates[].metadata.name must be identical strings. A mismatch causes a 'volume not found' error during Pod creation.

If kubectl get pvc shows STATUS as Pending, either no StorageClass exists or dynamic provisioning is disabled. Run kubectl get storageclass to check for a default StorageClass. In a minikube environment, the standard StorageClass is created automatically on minikube start.
If web-1 appears in kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w before web-0 reaches Running, you have likely created a Deployment by mistake. Verify that apiVersion is apps/v1 and kind is StatefulSet.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Validate Stable Identity and Data Persistence
tasks.goal: Write a marker file to a specific Pod (web-1) only, then forcibly delete the Pod. Confirm that the recreated Pod returns with the same name (web-1) and reattaches the same PVC (data-web-1), preserving the file.
tasks.prompt:
1. Use kubectl exec to create a file inside the web-1 container.
   - Write 'I am web-1' to /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt.
2. Delete the Pod with kubectl delete pod web-1.
3. Watch with kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w until web-1 is recreated and Running again.
4. Use kubectl exec to read /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt on the recreated web-1 and confirm the content is preserved.
5. Use the same command to read /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt on web-0 and confirm the file does not exist there (nothing was written to web-0).
tasks.starter:
# 1. Write a marker file to web-1
kubectl exec web-1 -- sh -c '____'

# Verify immediately
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat ____

# 2. Delete the Pod
kubectl delete pod ____

# 3. Watch recreation
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w

# 4. Confirm data is preserved after recreation
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat ____

# 5. Confirm no file exists on web-0
kubectl exec web-0 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
tasks.solution:
# 1. Write a marker file to web-1
kubectl exec web-1 -- sh -c 'echo "I am web-1" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'

# Verify immediately
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Output: I am web-1

# 2. Delete the Pod
kubectl delete pod web-1
# Output: pod "web-1" deleted

# 3. Watch recreation
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w
# Sample output:
# web-0   1/1   Running             ...
# web-1   0/1   Terminating         ...
# web-1   0/1   ContainerCreating   ...
# web-1   1/1   Running             ...

# 4. Confirm data is preserved
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Expected output: I am web-1  (identical to before deletion)

# 5. Confirm no file on web-0
kubectl exec web-0 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Expected output: cat: can't open '/usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt': No such file or directory
# -> The data-web-0 volume is completely separate, so the file is absent — this is expected
tasks.explanation: When the StatefulSet recreates web-1, it always reattaches the data-web-1 PVC. Files stored on the volume outlive the Pod, so id.txt is preserved. By contrast, web-0 never had the file written to its data-web-0 volume, so id.txt is absent there. Verifying both results together proves that per-Pod volumes are fully isolated from each other. With a Deployment, a recreated Pod is not guaranteed to reattach the same volume, so data persistence of this kind cannot be relied upon.
hints: When using sh -c '...' in kubectl exec, any command that contains a redirection (>) must be wrapped in quotes so the redirection is processed inside the container, not by the local shell.
There may be a brief moment after deleting web-1 where the Pod is not listed at all. Using the -w flag watches for the Terminating → ContainerCreating → Running transition so you don't miss it.
If the cat output in step 4 is empty, the container may not be fully ready yet. Check kubectl get pod web-1 and wait until STATUS is Running and READY shows 1/1 before running exec again.

tasks.title: Advanced: Scale Down, Verify PVC Retention, and Confirm Data Recovery on Scale-Up
tasks.goal: Verify that Pods are terminated in reverse order (highest ordinal first) when scaling down from 3 to 1 replica. Confirm that PVCs persist even after the StatefulSet is scaled down. Scale back up to 3 and verify that the original data is recovered.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a distinct marker file to each of web-0, web-1, and web-2.
   - web-0: echo "pod0" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
   - web-1: echo "pod1" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
   - web-2: echo "pod2" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
2. Run kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=1 to scale down. Watch with kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w and confirm that web-2 terminates before web-1.
3. Run kubectl get pvc and confirm that all three PVCs remain after the scale-down.
4. Run kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=3 to scale back up.
5. Read id.txt from the recreated web-1 and web-2 and confirm that the values pod1 and pod2 written before the scale-down have been recovered.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Write marker files to each Pod
kubectl exec web-0 -- sh -c 'echo "pod0" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'
kubectl exec web-1 -- sh -c 'echo "pod1" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'
kubectl exec web-2 -- sh -c 'echo "pod2" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'

# 2. Scale down to 1 replica (recommended: run watch in a separate terminal)
kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=____
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w

# 3. Confirm PVC retention
kubectl get pvc

# 4. Scale back up to 3 replicas
kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=____

# 5. Confirm data recovery after scale-up
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat ____
kubectl exec web-2 -- cat ____
tasks.solution:
# 1. Write marker files to each Pod
kubectl exec web-0 -- sh -c 'echo "pod0" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'
kubectl exec web-1 -- sh -c 'echo "pod1" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'
kubectl exec web-2 -- sh -c 'echo "pod2" > /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt'

# 2. Scale down to 1 replica
kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=1

# Watch for reverse-order termination
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx -w
# Expected output:
# web-2   1/1   Terminating   ...  <- terminates first
# web-2   0/1   Terminating   ...
# web-2         (gone)
# web-1   1/1   Terminating   ...  <- terminates next
# web-1   0/1   Terminating   ...
# web-1         (gone)
# web-0   1/1   Running       ...  <- only web-0 remains

# 3. Confirm PVC retention (StatefulSet does not delete them)
kubectl get pvc
# Expected output: data-web-0, data-web-1, data-web-2 all remain Bound

# 4. Scale back up to 3 replicas
kubectl scale statefulset web --replicas=3

# Wait for all Pods to be ready
kubectl wait pod -l app=nginx --for=condition=Ready --timeout=120s

# 5. Confirm data recovery
kubectl exec web-1 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Expected output: pod1

kubectl exec web-2 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Expected output: pod2

# Verify web-0 as well
kubectl exec web-0 -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/id.txt
# Expected output: pod0
tasks.explanation: StatefulSet scale-down terminates Pods from the highest ordinal (web-2) downward, one at a time. This ordering is designed to protect the Pod with ordinal 0 — typically the master — until the very end. PVCs created from volumeClaimTemplates are intentionally not deleted when the StatefulSet is scaled down or deleted, in order to protect the data they hold. When you scale back up, web-1 and web-2 reattach their existing data-web-1 and data-web-2 PVCs and recover the previous data. To fully clean up the PVCs, you must delete them explicitly with kubectl delete pvc data-web-0 data-web-1 data-web-2 — this permanently destroys the data, so proceed with caution.
hints: If the watch output shows termination in the order web-1 → web-2 (lowest ordinal first), you may be looking at a Deployment rather than a StatefulSet. Run kubectl get statefulset to confirm the web StatefulSet exists.
If kubectl exec web-1 returns 'error: pod not found' after scaling back up, the Pod is not yet Running. Check kubectl get pod web-1 and wait until READY shows 1/1 before retrying.

If id.txt in step 5 is empty or contains an unexpected value, inspect kubectl get pvc data-web-1 -o yaml and check spec.volumeName. The PVC must point to the same underlying volume as before the scale-down for data recovery to work. In a minikube environment using hostPath volumes, data recovery is straightforward to observe.
